english: also či (ci) alone: by all means, at all events, spyan či draṅs (spyan ci drangs) he must be conducted here at all events Glr. Comp. and deriv. či-ga (ci ga) what? col. — či-dgar (ci dgar), či dgá-bar (ci dga' bar) whatever one may wish, at pleasure, ad libitum. — či snyed (ci snyed) v. snyed (snyed). — *či toṅ* (*ci tong*) (lit. ytoṅ (gtong)) *žig* (*zhig*) some, something col. — či lta-bu (ci lta bu) of what sort, manner, fashion, quality or nature? Lat. qualis. — či ltar (ci ltar) how? in what manner? what? da či ltar bya (da ci ltar bya), W. *da či čo-če* (*da ci co ce*), what is now to be done? — či ltar gyúr-pai ytam byás-so (ci ltar gyur pa'i gtam byas so) he related what had happened, frq. — či-ste (ci ste), followed by na (na) or (rarely) te (te), in most cases = the Lat. sin, but if, if however; even supposed that; sometimes for gál-te (gal te), if, in case. — či sto (ci sto) what does it matter? ši yaṅ či sto (shi yang ci sto) if he dies, what does it matter? Thgy. (cf. čaṅ (cang)). — či-dra-ba (ci 'dra ba) similar to what? of what kind? also: of whatever description it may be Glr. — či-nas (ci nas) from which or what? out of which or what? by which? etc. (Bal.: *či-ne* (*ci ne*) how?), či-nas kyaṅ (ci nas kyang) = čis kyaṅ (cis kyang) q.v. — či tsam (ci tsam) how much? B., W.; či tsam yod kyaṅ (ci tsam yod kyang) though he have ever so much Mil.; či tsám-du (ci tsam du) how far? to what distance? — či-tsug (ci tsug) Cs., col. *či-zug* (*ci zug*), *gá-zug* (*ga zug*) how? in what manner? — či žig (ci zhig) 1. what? what a? 2. some one, any one, something, anything; či žig-tu dgos (ci zhig tu dgos) for what (purpose) is it wanted? Dzl.; či žig-na (ci zhig na) once, one time, at any time Pth.; či žig-nas (ci zhig nas) after that, afterwards Pth. — či yaṅ (ci yang), či-aṅ (ci ang), čaṅ (cang) whatever, any thing, all kinds of things, *ṅul yọ-na tsóṅ-gyu či yaṅ yọ* (*ngul yod na tsong rgyu ci yang yod*) C. if there is money, you may sell any thing; followed by a negative: nothing. — či rigs-pa (ci rigs pa) adj., či rigs-par (ci rigs par) adv. 1. in some measure, to a certain degree; in part, partly Tar.; 2. of every sort Dzl. and elsewh. — či-la (ci la) why? wherefore? Glr., W. col.; also for the de čii ṕyir żé-na (de ci'i phyir zhe na) of B.; further it is used inst. of an affirmative; e. g. question: shall we get rice there? answer: *tob yiṅ* (*thob yin*); či-la mi ťob (*ci la mi thob*) of course, why not? *či-la žu* (*ci la zhu*) why! well!
(Hind. چلم ) 1. the bowl of a hukka (water-pipe). — 2. a hukka.

enclitic, a modification of gčig (gcig), after s (s) usually changed into šig (shig), after vowels, and the liquids ṅ (ng), n (n), m (m), r (r), l (l) into žig (zhig) (exceptions, however, in provincialisms and in literature are not unfrequent) 1. after nouns, the indefinite article a, or a few, when following after a plural; sometimes also untranslatable: bud-méd-dag čig (bud med dag cig) some women; maṅ-po žig (mang po zhig) many (sometimes expressly opp. to maṅ-po (mang po), the many, Tar. 7, 15); gaṅ žig (gang zhig) v. gaṅ (gang); a little, some, šiṅ žig t'ú-ru gro dgos (shing zhig thu ru gro dgos) I must go and pick up some fire-wood Mil.; after infinitives: khrims daṅ gál-ba žig byéd-pa (khrims dang 'gal ba zhig byed pa) to commit a trespass, to make one's self guilty of a transgression Dzl.; tse p'ós-pa graṅs-med-pa žig myaṅ (tshe phos pa grangs med pa zhig myang) he suffered innumerable deaths Dzl.; it is even added to numerals, and not only when 'nearly', 'about' or similar words leave a given number undefined (mi lṅa tsam žig (mi lnga tsam zhig) some five people), but also in sentences like the following: čú-mig bži žig yod (chu mig bzhi zhig yod) there are four springs or fountains. In all these cases, however, it may also be omitted. The numeral for 'one' ought always to be written gčig (gcig) and never čig (cig), but prefixing the g (g) is so often neglected (e g. in t́abs čig-tu (thabs cig tu), lhan čig (lhan cig) etc.) that even grammarians let it pass. — 2. when affixed to verbs (to the root of the imp. mood, or, in negative sentences, to the root of the present tense) it is a sign of the imperative. In ancient literature it is used without reference to rank, whether it be in making prayers to Buddha, or in giving orders to a servant; at present in C. only in the latter way; in W. it is of rare occurrence.

a gerundial particle, the initial letter of which is changed acc. to the rules obtaining for čig (cig); it corresponds to the English participle in ing, is used in sentences beginning with when, after, as, and is affixed to verbal roots and adjectives, in the latter case including the auxiliary verb to be: ysón-por dúr-du bcúg-čiṅ bui ša zá-bar (gson por dur du bcug cing bu'i sha za bar)
